title: Understanding over-squashing and bottlenecks on graphs via curvature

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - UNDERSTANDING OVER SQUASHING AND BOTTLENECKS ON GRAPHS VIA CURVATURE

**Jake Topping**[12] _[†]_ **, Francesco Di Giovanni**[3] _[†]_ **, Benjamin P. Chamberlain**[3] **, Xiaowen Dong**[1] **, and Michael M. Bronstein**[23] 1University of Oxford 2Imperial College London 3Twitter

## ABSTRACT

Most graph neural networks (GNNs) use the message passing paradigm, in which node features are propagated on the input graph. Recent works pointed to the distortion of information flowing from distant nodes as a factor limiting the efficiency of message passing for tasks relying on long-distance interactions. This phenomenon, referred to as ‘over-squashing’, has been heuristically attributed to graph bottlenecks where the number of _k_ -hop neighbors grows rapidly with _k_ . We provide a precise description of the over-squashing phenomenon in GNNs and analyze how it arises from bottlenecks in the graph. For this purpose, we introduce a new edge-based combinatorial curvature and prove that negatively curved edges are responsible for the over-squashing issue. We also propose and experimentally test a curvature-based graph rewiring method to alleviate the over-squashing.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

In the past few years, deep learning on graphs and in particular graph neural networks (GNNs) (Sperduti, 1994; Goller & Kuchler, 1996; Sperduti & Starita, 1997; Frasconi et al., 1998; Gori et al., 2005; Scarselli et al., 2008; Bruna et al., 2014; Defferrard et al., 2016; Kipf & Welling, 2017; Gilmer et al., 2017) have become very popular in the machine learning community due to their ability to deal with broad classes of systems of relations and interactions, ranging from social networks to particle physics (Shlomi et al., 2021).

The vast majority of GNNs follow the _message passing_ paradigm (Gilmer et al.,

Figure 1: Top: evolution of curvature on a surface may reduce the bottleneck. Bottom: this paper shows how the same may be done on graphs to improve GNN performance. Blue/red shows negative/positive curvature.

2017), using learnable non-linear functions to diffuse information on the graph. Multiple popular GNN architectures such as GCN (Kipf & Welling, 2017) and GAT (Veliˇckovi´c et al., 2018) can be posed as particular flavors of this scheme and considered instances of a more general framework of geometric deep learning (Bronstein et al., 2021).

Some of the drawbacks of the message passing paradigm have now been identified and formalized, including the limits of expressive power (Xu et al., 2019; Morris et al., 2019; Maron et al., 2019) and the problem of over-smoothing (NT & Maehara, 2019; Oono & Suzuki, 2020). On the other hand, much less is known about the phenomenon of _over-squashing_ , consisting in the distortion of messages being propagated from distant nodes. Alon & Yahav (2021) proposed rewiring the graph as a way of reducing the _bottleneck_ , defined as those topological properties in the graph leading to over-squashing. This approach is in line with multiple other results e.g. using connectivity diffusion (Klicpera et al., 2019) as a preprocessing step to facilitate graph learning. Yet, the exact understanding of the over-squashing and how it originates from the bottlenecks in the topology of the underlying

graph are still elusive. Consequently, there is currently no consensus on the right method (either based on graph rewiring or not) to address the bottleneck and hence alleviate the over-squashing.

In this paper, we address these questions using tools from differential geometry, which traditionally is concerned with the study of manifolds. It offers an appealing framework to study the properties of graphs, in particular arguing that graphs, like manifolds, exhibit _curvature_ that makes them more suitable to be realized in spaces with hyperbolic geometry (Liu et al., 2019; Chami et al., 2019; Boguna et al., 2021). One notion of curvature that has received attention for graph learning is _Ricci curvature_ (Hamilton, 1988), also known in geometry for its use in Ricci flow and the subsequent proof of the Poincaré conjecture (Perelman, 2003). Certain graph analogues of the Ricci curvature (Forman, 2003; Ollivier, 2009; Sreejith et al., 2016) were used in Ni et al. (2018) for a discrete version of Ricci flow to construct a metric between graphs. Graph Ricci flow was also used in Ni et al. (2019) for community detection. Both of these methods use the edge weights as a substitute for the metric of a manifold, and do not change the topological structure of the graph.

**Contributions and Outline.** This paper, to our knowledge, is the first theoretical study of the bottleneck and over-squashing phenomena in message passing neural networks from a geometric perspective. In Section 2, we propose the Jacobian of node representations as a formal way of measuring the over-squashing and we show that the graph topology may compromise message propagation in graph neural networks by creating a bottleneck. In Section 3, we investigate how such a bottleneck is induced which leads to the over-squashing of information. To this aim, we introduce a new combinatorial edge-based curvature called Balanced Forman curvature that constitutes a sharp lower bound to the standard Ollivier curvature on graphs, and prove that negatively curved edges are responsible for the formation of bottlenecks (and hence for over-squashing). In Section 4, we present a new curvature-based method for graph rewiring called Stochastic Discrete Ricci Flow. According to the theoretical results in Section 3, this rewiring method is suited to address the graph bottleneck and hence alleviate the over-squashing by surgically targeting the edges responsible for the issue. By contrast, we rigorously show that a recently introduced diffusion-based rewiring scheme might generally fail to reduce the bottleneck. Finally, in Section 5, we compare different rewiring strategies experimentally on several standard graph learning datasets.

## 2.2 THE OVER-SQUASHING PROBLEM

Multiple recent papers observed that MPNNs tend to perform poorly in situations when the learned task requires long-range dependencies and at the same time the structure of the graph results in exponentially many long-range neighboring nodes. We say that a graph learning problem has _long-range dependencies_ when the output of a MPNN depends on representations of distant nodes interacting with each other. If long-range dependencies are present, messages coming from nonadjacent nodes need to be propagated across the network without being too distorted. In many cases however (e.g. in ‘small-world’ graphs such as social networks), the size of the receptive field _Br_ ( _i_ ) grows exponentially with _r_ . If this occurs, representations of exponentially many neighboring nodes need to be compressed into fixed-size vectors to propagate messages to node _i_ , causing a phenomenon referred to as _over-squashing_ of information (Alon & Yahav, 2021). In line with Alon & Yahav (2021), we refer to those structural properties of the graph that lead to over-squashing as a _bottleneck_[1] .

The sensitivity analysis in Lemma 1 relates the over-squashing – as measured by the Jacobian of the node representations – to the graph topology via powers of the augmented normalized adjacency matrix. In the next section we explore this connection further by analyzing which local properties of the graph structure affect the right hand side in equation 2, hence causing the bottleneck. We will address this problem by introducing a new combinatorial notion of edge-based curvature and showing that _negatively curved edges are those responsible for the over-squashing phenomenon_ .

## 3 GRAPH CURVATURE AND BOTTLENECK

A natural object in Riemannian geometry is the _Ricci curvature_ , a bilinear form determining the geodesic dispersion, i.e. whether geodesics starting at nearby points with ‘same’ velocity remain parallel ( _Euclidean space_ ), converge ( _spherical space_ ), or diverge ( _hyperbolic space_ ). To motivate the introduction of a Ricci curvature for graphs, we focus on these three cases. Consider two nodes _i ∼ j_ and two edges starting at _i_ and _j_ respectively. In a discrete spherical geometry (Figure 2a), the edges would meet at _k_ to form a triangle (complete graph). In a discrete Euclidean geometry (Figure 2b), the edges would stay parallel and form a 4-cycle based at _i ∼ j_ (orthogonal grid). Finally, in a discrete hyperbolic geometry (Figure 2c), the mutual distance of the edge endpoints would have grown compared to that of _i_ and _j_ (tree). Therefore, a Ricci curvature for graphs should provide us with more sophisticated tools than the degree to analyze the neighborhood of an edge.

> 1We note that the over-squashing issue is different from the problem of under-reaching; the latter simply amounts to a MPNN failing to fully explore a graph when the depth is smaller than the diameter (Barceló et al., 2019). The over-squashing phenomenon instead may occur even in deep GNNs with the number of layers larger than the graph diameter, as tested experimentally in Alon & Yahav (2021).
> 2The Jacobian of a GNN-output was also used by Xu et al. (2018) to set a similarity score among nodes.

The theory on _κ_ ( _i, j_ ) instead is richer (Lin et al., 2011; Münch, 2019) but its formulation makes it hard to control local quantities.

**Curvature and over-squashing.** Thanks to our new combinatorial curvature and the sensitivity analysis in Lemma 1, we are able to relate local curvature properties to the Jacobian of the node representations. This leads to one of the main results of this paper: **negatively curved edges are those causing the graph bottleneck and thus leading to the over-squashing phenomenon:**

**Theorem 4.** _Consider a MPNN as in equation 1. Let i ∼ j with di ≤ dj and assume that:_

- _(i) |∇φℓ| ≤ α and |∇ψℓ| ≤ β for each_ 0 _≤ ℓ ≤ L −_ 1 _, with L ≥_ 2 _the depth of the MPNN._
_Then there exists Qj ⊂ S_ 2( _i_ ) _satisfying |Qj| > δ[−]_[1] _and for_ 0 _≤ ℓ_ 0 _≤ L −_ 2 _we have_

Condition (i) is always satisfied and allows us to control the message passing functions. The requirement (ii) instead means that the curvature of ( _i, j_ ) is negative enough when compared to the degrees of _i_ and _j_ (recall that Ric( _i, j_ ) _> −_ 2). The further condition on _γ_ max is to avoid pathological cases where we have a large number of degenerate 4-cycles passing through the same three nodes.

To understand the conclusions in Theorem 4, let us fix _ℓ_ 0 = 0. The equation 4 shows that negatively curved edges are the ones causing bottlenecks, interpreted as how the graph topology prevents a representation _h_[(2)] _k_ to be affected by _non_ -adjacent features _h_[(0)] _i_ = _xi_ . Theorem 4 implies that if we have a negatively curved edge as in (ii), then there exist a large number of nodes _k_ such that GNNs - on average - struggle to propagate messages from _i_ to _k_ in two layers despite these nodes _k_ being at distance 2 from _i_ . In this case the over-squashing occurs as measured by the Jacobian in equation 4 and hence the propagation of information suffers. If the task at hand has long-range dependencies, then the over-squashing caused by the negatively curved edges may compromise the performance.

**Bottleneck via Cheeger constant.** We now relate the previous discussion about bottlenecks and curvature to spectral properties of the graph. In particular, since the spectral gap of a graph can be interpreted as a topological obstruction to the graph being partitioned into two communities, we argue below that this quantity is related to the graph bottleneck and should hence be controllable by the curvature. We start with an intuitive explanation: suppose we are given a graph _G_ with two communities separated by few edges. In this case, we see that the graph can be easily disconnected. This property is encoded in the classical notion of the _Cheeger constant_ (Chung & Graham, 1997)
where _∂S_ = _{_ ( _i, j_ ) : _i ∈ S, j ∈ V \ S}_ and vol( _S_ ) =[�] _i∈S[d][i]_[.][The main result about the Cheeger] constant is the _Cheeger inequality_ (Cheeger, 2015; Chung & Graham, 1997):

where _λ_ 1 is the first non-zero eigenvalue of the normalized graph Laplacian, often referred to as the _spectral gap_ . A graph with two tightly connected communities ( _S_ and _V \ S_ := _S_[¯] ) and few inter-community edges has a small Cheeger constant _hG_ . For nodes in different communities to interact with each other, all messages need to go through the same few _bridges_ hence leading to the over-squashing of information (a similar intuition was explored in Alon & Yahav (2021)). Therefore, _hG_ can be interpreted as a rough measure of graph ‘bottleneckedness’, in the sense that the smaller its value, the more likely the over-squashing is to occur across inter-community edges. Since Theorem 4 implies that negatively curved edges induce the bottleneck, we expect a relationship between _hG_ and the curvature of the graph. The next proposition follows from Theorem 2 and Lin et al. (2011):

**Proposition 5.** _If_ Ric( _i, j_ ) _≥ k >_ 0 _for all i ∼ j, then λ_ 1 _/_ 2 _≥ hG ≥[k]_ 2 _[.]_

Therefore, a positive lower bound on the curvature gives us a control on _hG_ and hence on the spectral gap of the graph. In the next section, we show that diffusion-based graph-rewiring methods might fail to significantly alter _hG_ and hence correct the graph bottleneck potentially induced by inter-community edges. This will lead us to propose an alternative curvature-based graph rewiring.

## 4 CURVATURE-BASED REWIRING METHODS

The traditional paradigm of message passing graph neural networks assumes that messages are propagated on the input graph (Gilmer et al., 2017). More recently, there is a trend to decouple the input graph from the graph used for information propagation. This can take the form of graph subsampling or resampling to deal with scalability (Hamilton et al., 2017) or topological noise (Zhang et al., 2019), using larger motif-based (Monti et al., 2018) or multi-hop filters (Rossi et al., 2020), or changing the graph either as a preprocessing step (Klicpera et al., 2019; Alon & Yahav, 2021) or adaptively for the downstream task (Wang et al., 2019; Kazi et al., 2020). Such methods are often generically referred to as _graph rewiring_ .

In the context of this paper, we assume that graph rewiring attempts to produce a new graph _G[′]_ = ( _V, E[′]_ ) with a different edge structure that reduces the bottleneck and hence potentially alleviates the over-squashing of information. We propose a method that leverages the graph curvature to guide the rewiring steps in a surgical way by modifying the negatively-curved edges, so to decrease the bottleneck without significantly compromising the statistical properties of the input graph. We also rigorously show that a random-walk based rewiring method might generally fail to obtain an edge set _E[′]_ with a significant improvement in its bottleneckedness as measured by the Cheeger constant.

**Curvature-based graph rewiring.** Since according to Theorem 4 negatively curved edges induce a bottleneck and are hence responsible for over-squashing, a curvature-based rewiring method should attempt to alleviate a graph’s strongly-negatively curved edges. To this end we implement a simple rewiring method called Stochastic Discrete Ricci Flow (SDRF), described in Algorithm 1.

## **Algorithm 1:** Stochastic Discrete Ricci Flow (SDRF)

**Input:** graph _G_ , temperature _τ >_ 0, max number of iterations, optional Ric upper-bound _C_[+] **Repeat**

1) For edge _i ∼ j_ with minimal Ricci curvature Ric( _i, j_ ):

Calculate vector _**x**_ where _xkl_ = Ric _kl_ ( _i, j_ ) _−_ Ric( _i, j_ ), the improvement to Ric( _i, j_ ) from adding edge _k ∼ l_ where _k ∈ B_ 1( _i_ ), _l ∈ B_ 1( _j_ ); Sample index _k, l_ with probability softmax( _τ_ _**x**_ ) _kl_ and add edge _k ∼ l_ to _G_ .
2) Remove edge _i ∼ j_ with maximal Ricci curvature Ric( _i, j_ ) if Ric( _i, j_ ) _> C_[+] . **Until** convergence, or max iterations reached;

At each iteration this preprocessing step adds an edge to ‘support’ the graph’s most negatively curved edge, and then removes the most positively curved edge. The requirement on the added edge _k ∼ l_ that _k ∈ B_ 1( _i_ ) and _l ∈ B_ 1( _j_ ) ensures that we’re adding either an extra 3- or 4-cycle around the negative edge _i ∼ j_ so that this is a local modification. The graph edit distance between the original and preprocessed graph is bounded above by 2 _×_ the max number of iterations. The temperature _τ_ determines how stochastic the edge addition is, with _τ_ = _∞_ being fully deterministic (the best edge is always added). At each step we remove the edge with most positive curvature to balance the distributions of curvature and node degrees. We use Balanced Forman curvature as in equation 3 for Ric( _i, j_ ). _C_[+] can be chosen to stop the method skewing the curvature distribution negative, including _C_[+] = _∞_ to not remove any edges. The method is inspired by the continuous (backwards) Ricci flow with the aim of homogenizing edge curvatures. This is different from more direct extensions of Ricci flow on graphs where it becomes increasingly expensive to propagate messages across negatively curved edges (as in other applications such as Ni et al. (2019)). An example alongside its continuous analogue can be seen in Figure 1.

**Can random-walk based rewiring address bottlenecks?** A good way of understanding the effectiveness of SDRF in reducing the graph bottleneck is through comparison with random-walk based rewiring strategies. Recall that, as argued in Section 3, the Cheeger constant _hG_ of a graph constitutes a rough measure of its bottleneckedness as induced by the inter-community edges (a small _hG_ is indicative of a bottleneck). Suppose we are given a graph _G_ with a small _hG_ and wish to rewire it into a graph _G[′]_ with a significantly improved Cheeger constant in order to reduce the inter-community bottleneck. A random-walk based rewiring method such as DIGL (Klicpera et al., 2019) acts by smoothing out the graph adjacency and hence tends to promote connections among nodes at short

The property that the new Cheeger constant is directly controlled by the old one stems from the fact that a random-walk approach like in Klicpera et al. (2019) is meant to act more relevantly on intra-community edges rather than inter-community edges because it prioritizes short diffusion distance nodes. This is also why this method performs well on high-homophily datasets, as discussed below. In particular, for a fixed _α ∈_ (0 _,_ 1), the bound in Theorem 6 can be very small. As a specific example, consider two complete graphs _Kn_ joined by one bridge. Then _hG_ = ( _n_ ( _n −_ 1) + 1) _[−]_[1] , which means that the bound on the right hand side is _O_ ( _n[−]_[2] ).

Theorem 6 implies that a diffusion approach such as DIGL might fail to yield a new edge set _E[′]_ with a sufficiently improved bottleneck. By contrast, from Theorem 4 and Proposition 5, we deduce that a curvature-based rewiring method such as SDRF properly addresses the edges that cause the bottleneck.

**Graph structure preservation.** Although a graph-rewiring approach aims at providing a new edge set _E[′]_ potentially more beneficial for the given learning task, it is still desirable to control how far _E[′]_ is from _E_ . In this regard, we note that a curvature-based rewiring is surgical in nature and hence more likely to preserve the structure of the input graph better than a random-walk based approach. Consider, for example, that we are given _ρ >_ 0 and wish to rewire the graph such that the new edge set _E[′]_ is within graph-edit distance _ρ_ from the original _E_ . Theorem 4 tells us how to do the rewiring under such constraints in order to best address the over-squashing: the topological modifications need to be localized _around the most negatively-curved edges_ . We can do this with SDRF, with the maximum number of iterations set to _ρ/_ 2.

Secondly, we also point out that Ric( _i, j_ ) _< −_ 2 + _δ_ implies that min _{di, dj} >_ 2 _/δ_ . Therefore, if we mostly modify the edge set at those nodes _i, j_ joined by an edge with large negative curvature, then we are perturbing nodes with high degree where such a change is relatively insignificant, and thus overall statistical properties of the rewired graph such as degree distribution are likely to be better preserved. Moreover, graph convolutional networks tend to be more stable to perturbations of high degree nodes (Zügner et al., 2020; Kenlay et al., 2021), making curvature-based rewiring more suitable for the downstream learning tasks with popular GNN architectures.

**Homophily and bottleneck.** As a final remark, note that the graph rewiring techniques considered in this paper (both DIGL and SDRF) are based purely on the topological structure of the graph and completely agnostic to the node features and to whether the dataset is _homophilic_ (adjacent nodes have same labels) or _heterophilic_ . Nonetheless, the different nature of these rewiring methods allows us to draw a few broad conclusions about their suitability in each of these settings. A random-walk

approach such as DIGL tends to improve the connectivity among nodes that are at short diffusion distance; since for a high-homophily dataset these nodes often share the same label, a rewiring method like DIGL is likely to act as _graph denoising_ and yield improved performance. On the other hand, for datasets with low homophily, nodes at short diffusion distance are more likely to belong to different label classes, meaning that a diffusion-based rewiring might inject noise and hence compromise performance as also noted in Klicpera et al. (2019). Conversely, on a low-homophily dataset, a curvature-based approach as SDRF modifies the edge set mainly around the most negatively curved edges, meaning that it decreases the bottleneck without significantly increasing the connectivity among nodes with different labels. In fact, long-range dependencies are often more relevant in low-homophily settings, where nodes sharing the same labels are in general not neighbors. This observation is largely confirmed by experimental results reported in the next section.

## 5 EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS

**Experiment setup.** To demonstrate the theoretical results in this paper we ran a suite of semisupervised node classification tasks comparing our curvature-based rewiring method SDRF to DIGL from Klicpera et al. (2019) (GDC with the PPR kernel) and the +FA method from Alon & Yahav (2021), where the last layer of the GNN is made fully connected. We evaluate the methods on nine datasets: Cornell, Texas and Wisconsin from the WebKB dataset[4] ; Chameleon and Squirrel (Rozemberczki et al., 2021) along with Actor (Tang et al., 2009); and Cora (McCallum et al., 2000), Citeseer (Sen et al., 2008) and Pubmed (Namata et al., 2012). Statistics for these datasets can be found in Appendix F.1. Our base model is a GCN (Kipf & Welling, 2017). Following Shchur et al. (2018) and Klicpera et al. (2019) we optimized hyperparameters for all dataset-preprocessing combinations separately by random search over 100 data splits. Results are reported as average accuracies on a test set used once with 95% confidence intervals calculated by bootstrapping. We compared the performance on graphs with no preprocessing, making the graph undirected, +FA, DIGL, SDRF, and the given combinations. For DIGL + Undirected we symmetrized the diffusion matrix as in Klicpera et al. (2019), and for SDRF + Undirected we made the graph undirected before applying SDRF. For more details on the experiments and datasets see Appendix F, and for the hyperparameters used for each model and preprocessing see Appendix F.4.

**Node classification results.** Table 2 shows the results of the experiments. As well as reporting results we give a measure of homophily _H_ ( _G_ ) proposed by Pei et al. (2019) (restated in Appendix F, equation 21), by which we can see our experiment set is diverse with respect to homophily. We see that SDRF improves upon the baseline in all cases, and that the largest improvements are seen on the low-homophily datasets. We also see that SDRF matches or outperforms DIGL and +FA on most datasets, supporting our argument that curvature-based rewiring is a viable candidate for improving GNN performance.

**Graph topology change.** Furthermore, SDRF preserves the graph topology to a far greater extent than DIGL due to its surgical nature. Table 3 shows the number of edges added / removed by the two preprocessings on each dataset as a percentage of the original number of edges. We see that for optimal performance DIGL makes the graph much denser, which significantly affects the node degrees and may negatively impact the time and space complexity of the downstream GNN, which typically are _O_ ( _E_ ). In comparison, SDRF adds and removes a similar number of edges and approximately preserves the degree distribution. The effect on the full degree distribution for three

## 6 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we studied the graph bottleneck and the over-squashing phenomena limiting the performance of message passing graph neural networks from a geometric perspective. We started with a Jacobian approach to determine how the over-squashing phenomenon is dictated by the graph topology as in equation 2. We then investigated further how the topology induces the bottleneck and hence causes over-squashing. We introduced a new notion of edge-based Ricci curvature called Balanced Forman curvature, relating it to the classical Ollivier curvature (Theorem 2). We then proved in Theorem 4 that negatively-curved edges are responsible for over-squashing, calling for a possibility of curvature-based rewiring of the graph in order to improve its bottleneckedness. We show one such possibility (Algorithm 1), inspired by the classical Ricci flow and comment on the advantages of surgical method such as this. We show both theoretically and experimentally that the proposed method can be advantageous compared to a diffusion-based rewiring approach, opening the door for curvature-based rewiring methods for improving GNN performance going forward.

**Limitations and future directions.** Our paper establishes a geometric perspective on the graph bottleneck and over-squashing, providing new tools to study and cope with these phenomena. One limitation of our work is that the theoretical results presented here do not currently extend to multigraphs. In addition, the current methodology is agnostic to information beyond the graph topology, such as node features. In future works, we will develop a notion of the curvature and the corresponding rewiring method that can take into account such information.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper makes a compelling conceptual contribution by linking graph curvature to over-squashing in GNNs, introducing a new combinatorial curvature measure and proposing a theoretically grounded rewiring method. The theoretical framework is elegant, connecting differential geometry to message passing dynamics via Jacobian sensitivity and spectral properties. The experimental results suggest practical benefits, especially on low-homophily graphs, and the analysis of degree distribution preservation strengthens the appeal of the proposed method.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient justification for Balanced Forman curvature design: The paper introduces 'Balanced Forman curvature' as a new edge-based combinatorial curvature but does not sufficiently justify its specific functional form, particularly the weighting scheme involving max/min degrees and the inverse degeneracy factor. While Theorem 2 shows it lower-bounds Ollivier curvature, no ablation or comparison is provided to demonstrate why this particular balance improves over existing Forman or Ollivier variants in detecting bottlenecks. Given that recent work (e.g., 'Rethinking the Gold Standard', 2026) critiques discrete curvature measures for missing over-squashing cases, the authors should validate that their curvature definition captures bottleneck edges more accurately than alternatives, or at least discuss its limitations in non-homogeneous or high-girth graphs.

- Circularity in curvature-based rewiring without causal validation: The proposed Stochastic Discrete Ricci Flow (SDRF) uses the same curvature measure that is claimed to cause over-squashing to guide rewiring, creating a risk of circular reasoning. The experiments show improved performance but do not isolate whether the gains come from reduced over-squashing or other topological side effects (e.g., degree regularization, sparsity control). A more convincing validation would include direct measurement of Jacobian sensitivity before and after rewiring, or synthetic tasks explicitly requiring long-range information flow, to confirm that performance gains are mediated by improved information propagation rather than architectural side benefits.

- Lack of ablation for rewiring mechanism components: The SDRF algorithm includes multiple design choices: adding edges near negatively curved ones, removing highly positive edges, and the softmax temperature parameter. However, the paper provides no ablation study to assess the contribution of each component. For example, it is unclear whether removing positive-curvature edges helps performance or whether a simpler strategy—adding edges around negative-curvature bridges—would suffice. Without such analysis, it is hard to determine which aspects of the method are essential, limiting its interpretability and reproducibility.

- Overlooked counterexamples to negative curvature as necessary condition: The paper claims that negatively curved edges are responsible for over-squashing, but recent literature (e.g., 'Rethinking the Gold Standard', 2026) shows counterexamples where bottlenecks occur without negative curvature, such as in expander graphs or certain grid-like structures. The authors do not address these cases, nor do they clarify whether their theory applies only to specific graph families. This omission weakens the generality of the main theoretical claim and could mislead readers into assuming curvature is a universal bottleneck indicator.

- Inadequate scalability and complexity discussion: Although the authors note that Balanced Forman curvature has lower computational complexity than Ollivier curvature (O(|E|d_max^2) vs O(|E|d_max^3)), they do not evaluate the scalability of SDRF on large or real-world graphs. Real benchmarks like OGB or Reddit are absent, and the largest dataset used (Pubmed) has only 19K edges. Given that curvature computation and iterative rewiring could become prohibitive at scale, the paper should either demonstrate efficiency on larger graphs or discuss approximations (e.g., sampling, localized updates) to make the method viable beyond small benchmarks.

- Missing direct comparison to alternative rewiring methods targeting over-squashing: The experimental comparison is limited to DIGL and +FA, but recent methods like spectral-aware rewiring (Anonymous, ICML 2025) or higher-order message passing (Anonymous, arXiv 2024) directly target over-squashing and offer competing geometric or topological solutions. The absence of these baselines makes it difficult to assess the relative effectiveness of curvature-based rewiring. Including such comparisons would strengthen the paper’s claim to practical innovation.

- No ablation of curvature-based rewiring components: The paper proposes Stochastic Discrete Ricci Flow (SDRF) with multiple design choices: adding edges near negatively curved ones, removing highly positive edges, and using a softmax temperature for stochasticity. However, no ablation study isolates the impact of each component. For instance, it is unclear whether removing positive-curvature edges improves performance or merely preserves degree distribution, or whether deterministic edge addition (infinite temperature) performs worse. To validate the necessity of each mechanism, the authors should report results on key datasets (e.g., Wisconsin, Actor) with variants of SDRF: one that only adds edges without removal, one with fixed edge choices (τ = ∞), and one that targets different curvature thresholds. Without this, the core algorithmic contribution lacks empirical grounding.

- Missing direct evidence linking rewiring to reduced over-squashing: The paper claims SDRF alleviates over-squashing, but the experiments only measure downstream node classification accuracy, not the actual information flow. There is no direct measurement of Jacobian sensitivity—such as the average norm of ∂h_i^(L)/∂x_j for distant j—before and after rewiring. On a representative low-homophily dataset like Chameleon, the authors should compute this Jacobian for a GCN on the original and SDRF-preprocessed graph, showing that sensitivity to distant nodes increases post-rewiring. This would confirm that performance gains are due to improved propagation, not just better local connectivity or regularization.

- No synthetic test case demonstrating curvature’s role in over-squashing: The theoretical claims about negative curvature causing over-squashing are not verified on a controlled synthetic graph where bottlenecks are known and curvature can be manipulated. For example, the paper should construct a 'ladder' graph with two cliques connected by a single bridge edge—known to induce over-squashing—and compute Balanced Forman curvature on the bridge, showing it is negative. Then, train a shallow GNN to propagate information across the bridge and show both low Jacobian sensitivity and poor task performance. After applying SDRF to rewire the bridge, demonstrate that curvature increases and performance improves. This would ground the theory in a concrete, interpretable example.

- Lack of comparison to curvature alternatives on bottleneck detection: The paper introduces Balanced Forman curvature but does not compare its ability to identify bottleneck edges against other curvature notions like Ollivier or Augmented Forman on real or synthetic graphs. On a graph with known over-squashing (e.g., a tree-like hierarchy), the authors should compute all three curvatures and evaluate which best correlates with low Jacobian sensitivity from root to leaves. For instance, report precision-recall for detecting edges that, when removed, most degrade long-range communication. This would justify the new definition beyond theoretical bounds and show practical advantages in bottleneck localization.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Justify the design of Balanced Forman curvature with ablation or comparison to alternative curvature measures on bottleneck detection.

- Provide causal validation that SDRF improves performance by reducing over-squashing, using direct Jacobian or information flow metrics.

- Include ablation studies for SDRF components (e.g., edge removal, temperature) to clarify their individual contributions.

- Address known counterexamples where over-squashing occurs without negative curvature, clarifying the scope of the theoretical claims.

- Evaluate scalability on larger graphs or discuss practical approximations for real-world deployment.

comments.title: Incorrect implication about Ricci curvature's role in Poincaré conjecture
comments.quote: One notion of curvature that has received attention for graph learning is Ricci curvature *(Hamilton, 1988)*, also known in geometry for its use in Ricci flow and the subsequent proof of the Poincaré conjecture *(Perelman, 2003)*.
comments.explanation: The sentence misrepresents the role of Ricci curvature in the proof of the Poincaré conjecture. It was not the curvature itself that proved the conjecture, but the analytic behavior of the Ricci flow—a geometric evolution equation based on Ricci curvature—used by Perelman to establish the Thurston geometrization conjecture, which implies the Poincaré conjecture. The current phrasing risks misleading readers into thinking curvature is a direct logical tool in the proof, rather than a component of a dynamic process. This weakens the precision of a key analogy motivating the paper’s geometric framework.

comments.title: Circular definition of normalized adjacency matrix
comments.quote: Similarly we let $\hat{D}=D+I$, with $D$ the diagonal degree matrix, and let $\hat{A}=\hat{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}}\hat{A}\hat{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ be the normalized augmented adjacency matrix
comments.explanation: The equation $\hat{A} = \hat{D}^{-1/2} \hat{A} \hat{D}^{-1/2}$ is circular: $\hat{A}$ appears on both sides with the same symbol but different meanings. On the left, it denotes the normalized matrix; on the right, it refers to the unnormalized $A + I$. This creates an implicit matrix equation that only holds under restrictive conditions (e.g., regular graphs) and is generally invalid. The correct definition should explicitly distinguish the augmented adjacency $\tilde{A} = A + I$ and set $\hat{A} = \tilde{D}^{-1/2} \tilde{A} \tilde{D}^{-1/2}$ to avoid confusion and ensure mathematical soundness.

comments.title: Incorrect exponent in binary tree Jacobian bound
comments.quote: For example, if $d_{G}(i,s)=r+1$ and the sub-graph induced on $B_{r+1}(i)$ is a binary tree, then $(\hat{A}^{r+1})_{is}=2^{-1}3^{-r}$, which gives an exponential decay of the node dependence on input features at distance $r$, as also heuristically argued by *Alon and Yahav (2021)*.
comments.explanation: The claimed value $(\hat{A}^{r+1})_{is} = 2^{-1} 3^{-r}$ is inconsistent with the normalization $\hat{A} = \tilde{D}^{-1/2} (A + I) \tilde{D}^{-1/2}$. In a binary tree, internal nodes have degree 3 (after self-loop augmentation), so edge weights scale as $1/\sqrt{3} \cdot 1/\sqrt{3} = 1/3$. For a path of $r+1$ edges, the dominant contribution to $(\hat{A}^{r+1})_{is}$ is $O(3^{-(r+1)/2})$, not $3^{-r}$. The $2^{-1}$ factor lacks justification. This error mischaracterizes the rate of message attenuation and undermines the calibration of Lemma 1’s bound, which is central to the paper’s sensitivity analysis.

comments.title: Misattribution of over-squashing mechanism to normalization decay
comments.quote: For example, if $d_{G}(i,s)=r+1$ and the sub-graph induced on $B_{r+1}(i)$ is a binary tree, then $(\hat{A}^{r+1})_{is}=2^{-1}3^{-r}$, which gives an exponential decay of the node dependence on input features at distance $r$, as also heuristically argued by *Alon and Yahav (2021)*.
comments.explanation: The paper attributes exponential decay in $|\partial h_i^{(r+1)}/\partial x_s|$ to the normalization-induced attenuation captured by $(\hat{A}^{r+1})_{is}$, citing Alon and Yahav (2021). However, that work identifies over-squashing primarily with gradient dilution due to exponential growth in the number of $k$-hop neighbors, not with per-path attenuation from normalization. These are distinct mechanisms: one involves combinatorial explosion of paths, the other involves decay along individual paths. The example conflates them, misrepresenting both the cited literature and the dominant cause of over-squashing in expanders. The discussion should distinguish between these effects to maintain conceptual clarity.

comments.title: Tree curvature incorrectly expressed as global function
comments.quote: The curvature is negative when  $i \sim j$  behaves as a bridge between  $S_{1}(i)$  and  $S_{1}(j)$ , while it is positive when  $S_{1}(i)$  and  $S_{1}(j)$  stay connected after removing  $i \sim j$ . We refer to Ric as Balanced Forman curvature. We can relate the Balanced Forman curvature to the Jacobian of hidden features, while also extending many results valid for the Ollivier curvature  $\kappa(i,j)$  thanks to our next theorem.
comments.explanation: Table 1 lists the curvature of a tree $T_r$ as $4/(r+1) - 2$, which depends on the global radius $r$. This contradicts the local nature of curvature: for a fixed edge in a tree, the value should depend only on its immediate neighborhood, not on the size of the entire graph. For an internal edge in a binary tree with degrees 3 and 3, Definition 1 gives $\text{Ric}(i,j) = 2/3 + 2/3 - 2 = -2/3$, independent of $r$. The table entry falsely suggests curvature varies with tree size, undermining the geometric interpretation. The entry should be corrected to reflect the local formula $2/d_i + 2/d_j - 2$ for internal edges and 0 for leaf edges.

comments.title: 4-cycle term in curvature formula contradicts geometric analogy
comments.quote: In a discrete spherical geometry (Figure 2a), the edges would meet at $k$ to form a triangle (complete graph). In a discrete Euclidean geometry (Figure 2b), the edges would stay parallel and form a 4-cycle based at $i\sim j$ (orthogonal grid). Finally, in a discrete hyperbolic geometry (Figure 2c), the mutual distance of the edge endpoints would have grown compared to that of $i$ and $j$ (tree).
comments.explanation: The geometric analogy assigns zero curvature to 4-cycles (Euclidean), yet in Definition 1, the 4-cycle term $\frac{(\gamma_{\max})^{-1}}{\max\{d_i,d_j\}} (|\sharp_{\square}^{i}| + |\sharp_{\square}^{j}|)$ is added to $\text{Ric}(i,j)$, contributing positively. In a regular grid, this term increases curvature above the base $2/d_i + 2/d_j - 2$, potentially yielding positive values despite the intended flat geometry. This contradicts Figure 2b, which labels grids as zero curvature. Either the formula should neutralize the 4-cycle contribution in flat cases or the analogy must be revised to reflect that 4-cycles contribute positively but less than triangles.

comments.title: Incorrect application of Paeng's result for volume growth
comments.quote:
From Theorem 2 and Paeng (2012), we find:

Corollary 3. If  $\operatorname{Ric}(i,j) \geq k > 0$  for any edge  $i \sim j$ , then there exists a polynomial  $P$  such that

$$
\left| B _ {r} (i) \right| \leq P (r), \quad \forall i \in V.
$$
comments.explanation: Paeng (2012) studies compact Riemannian manifolds with positive Ricci curvature, which have bounded diameter—hence volume growth is trivially bounded. The corollary, however, claims polynomial growth as a consequence of positive curvature, which is not supported by that result. In discrete settings, positive Ollivier curvature (which dominates $\text{Ric}$ by Theorem 2) implies bounded diameter, not polynomial growth. For example, a cycle has zero curvature and linear growth, but a complete graph has positive curvature and constant ball size. The cited justification is incorrect. A proper reference would be to results on non-negative curvature implying at most polynomial growth, but even then, additional assumptions are needed.

comments.title: Curvature undefined for degree-1 edges contradicts bottleneck intuition
comments.quote:
we let  $\operatorname{Ric}(i,j)$  be zero if  $\min\{d_i,d_j\} = 1$  and otherwise

$$
\operatorname {R i c} (i, j) := \frac {2}{d _ {i}} + \frac {2}{d _ {j}} - 2 + 2 \frac {\left| \sharp_ {\Delta} (i , j) \right|}{\max  \left\{d _ {i} , d _ {j} \right\}} + \frac {\left| \sharp_ {\Delta} (i , j) \right|}{\min  \left\{d _ {i} , d _ {j} \right\}} + \frac {\left(\gamma_ {\max }\right) ^ {- 1}}{\max  \left\{d _ {i} , d _ {j} \right\}} \left(\left| \sharp_ {\square} ^ {i} \right| + \left| \sharp_ {\square} ^ {j} \right|\right), \tag {3}
$$
comments.explanation: Setting $\text{Ric}(i,j) = 0$ for $\min\{d_i,d_j\} = 1$ avoids negative values in star graphs, but it contradicts the paper’s thesis. In a star, edges from center to leaves are bottlenecks—messages between leaves must pass through the hub—yet they are assigned zero curvature. The formula for $\min\{d_i,d_j\} > 1$ would give positive curvature for large stars, suggesting anti-bottleneck behavior. This ad hoc rule undermines the claim that negative curvature identifies bottlenecks. A principled extension to degree-1 edges, or a justification based on geometric limits, is needed to maintain consistency.

comments.title: Incorrect Cheeger constant definition with scoping error
comments.quote: h_{G}:=\min_{S\subset V}h_{S},\quad h_{S}:=\min_{S\subset V}\frac{|\partial S|}{\min\{\textup{vol}(S),\textup{vol}(V\setminus S)\}}$ (5)
comments.explanation: The definition of $h_S$ contains a scoping error: it reuses $S$ as a bound variable in the minimization $\min_{S\subset V}$, despite $S$ being the argument of the function. This renders $h_S$ ill-defined. The correct definition is $h_S := \frac{|\partial S|}{\min\{\textup{vol}(S), \textup{vol}(V\setminus S)\}}$ with no inner minimization, and then $h_G := \min_{S\subset V} h_S$. The current formulation breaks the logical structure of the definition and could mislead readers about how the Cheeger constant is computed.

comments.title: Unjustified lower bound on Cheeger constant from curvature
comments.quote: If $\textup{Ric}(i,j)\geq k>0$ for all $i\sim j$, then $\lambda_{1}/2\geq h_{G}\geq\frac{k}{2}$.
comments.explanation: The claim $h_G \geq k/2$ relies on Lin et al. (2011), which establishes a Cheeger inequality for Ollivier curvature $\kappa(i,j) \geq k > 0$, not for $\text{Ric}(i,j)$. While Theorem 2 gives $\kappa \geq \text{Ric}$, the inequality in Lin et al. requires uniform lower bounds on $\kappa$ and additional geometric conditions (e.g., bounded diameter). Without these, the bound does not hold—e.g., a long path has positive $\kappa$ but $h_G \to 0$. The proposition overgeneralizes by omitting necessary assumptions, risking incorrect application to large or sparse graphs.

comments.title: False implication from curvature to node degree
comments.quote: Secondly, we also point out that $\text{Ric}(i,j)<-2+\delta$ implies that $\min\{d_{i},d_{j}\}>2/\delta$. Therefore, if we mostly modify the edge set at those nodes $i,j$ joined by an edge with large negative curvature, then we are perturbing nodes with high degree where such a change is relatively insignificant, and thus overall statistical properties of the rewired graph such as degree distribution are likely to be better preserved.
comments.explanation: The implication $\text{Ric}(i,j) < -2 + \delta \Rightarrow \min\{d_i, d_j\} > 2/\delta$ is false. For a bridge edge between two degree-2 nodes with no cycles, $\text{Ric}(i,j) = -2$, satisfying the left side for any $\delta > 0$, but $\min\{d_i, d_j\} = 2$. If $\delta = 0.1$, the right side requires $\min\{d_i, d_j\} > 20$, which fails. The argument incorrectly assumes high degree for negatively curved edges, undermining the claim that rewiring preserves degree distribution. This must be corrected or replaced with a valid justification.

comments.title: Implausible Wasserstein distances in degree distribution comparison
comments.quote:
$W_{1}$  (Original, DIGL)  $= 243.81$
$W_{1}$  (Original, SDRF)  $= 1.03$
comments.explanation: The reported Wasserstein-1 distances, such as 243.81 for DIGL on Actor, are implausibly large. For a graph with ~7,650 nodes and average degree ~6, even extreme rewiring (e.g., creating a star) would not produce $W_1$ values in the hundreds. A shift of one unit in average degree over $10^3$ nodes gives $W_1 \sim 1$. Values above 200 suggest computational error, misreporting (e.g., sum instead of average), or misunderstanding of the metric. In contrast, SDRF's $W_1 = 1.03$ is plausible. This discrepancy undermines the quantitative claim that SDRF better preserves topology.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for curvature update in SDRF
comments.quote:
Calculate vector $\bm{x}$ where $x_{kl}=\text{Ric}_{kl}(i,j)-\text{Ric}(i,j)$, the improvement to $\text{Ric}(i,j)$
from adding edge $k\sim l$ where $k\in B_1(i)$, $l\in B_1(j)$;
comments.explanation: The notation $\text{Ric}_{kl}(i,j)$ is undefined and ambiguous. It is clearly meant to denote the value of $\text{Ric}(i,j)$ after adding edge $k\sim l$, but the subscript $kl$ suggests it is a curvature associated with edge $(k,l)$, not a modified version of $\text{Ric}(i,j)$. This could mislead readers into interpreting it as a joint or transfer curvature. The correct interpretation—$\text{Ric}^{(G+\{k\sim l\})}(i,j)$—should be explicitly stated using superscript notation to denote graph modification.

comments.title: Unverified assumption in SDRF's bottleneck improvement
comments.quote: By contrast, from Theorem 4 and Proposition 5, we deduce that a curvature-based rewiring method such as SDRF properly addresses the edges that cause the bottleneck.
comments.explanation: The deduction assumes that modifying negatively curved edges via SDRF directly improves the Cheeger constant, but no theorem connects SDRF’s edits to a provable increase in $h_G$. Theorem 4 identifies negative curvature as *indicative* of bottlenecks, and Proposition 5 links curvature bounds to spectral gaps, but neither proves that SDRF’s edge additions reduce the global bottleneck. For example, adding an edge might improve $\text{Ric}(i,j)$ but create new bottlenecks elsewhere. The claim overstates the deductive link and should be tempered to reflect that SDRF targets *associated* edges, not proven causal ones.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces a novel geometric perspective on over-squashing in GNNs, proposing Balanced Forman curvature as a theoretical explanation and a curvature-based rewiring method (SDRF) as a solution. While the theoretical framework is elegant and the experiments show promising results, there are significant concerns about the completeness of the theoretical claims, the adequacy of experimental validation, and the framing of the contribution relative to existing work.

The paper makes a valuable theoretical contribution by formalizing the connection between negative curvature and over-squashing, providing a clear mathematical framework that advances understanding of this phenomenon. The proposed SDRF method is intuitive and shows good performance in experiments, particularly on low-homophily datasets. However, the paper overstates the necessity of negative curvature for over-squashing and lacks comprehensive experimental validation across diverse graph types and GNN architectures.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient justification of curvature necessity: The paper's central claim that negatively curved edges are responsible for over-squashing (Theorem 4) only shows sufficiency, not necessity. The literature context indicates recent work challenging whether negative curvature is necessary for over-squashing. The paper should acknowledge this limitation more clearly and either provide additional theoretical support for necessity or reframe the claim to emphasize that negative curvature is one important cause of over-squashing, not the exclusive cause. This matters because it affects the general applicability of the proposed solution and may mislead readers about the completeness of the explanation.

- Limited experimental validation of theoretical claims: The experiments focus only on node classification tasks with GCN architecture, failing to validate the theoretical claims across diverse scenarios mentioned in the literature context. Specifically, there are no experiments on: (1) tasks explicitly requiring long-range dependencies beyond node classification, (2) different GNN architectures (GAT, GraphSAGE) to test generalizability, (3) directed or heterogeneous graphs where the curvature notion may not apply, or (4) synthetic graphs designed to specifically test the curvature-over-squashing relationship. The paper should include experiments that directly measure information distortion (e.g., using the Jacobian metric from Lemma 1) to empirically validate the theoretical connection between curvature and over-squashing.

- Inadequate baseline comparisons: The experimental comparison omits important recent over-squashing mitigation methods mentioned in the literature context, particularly spectral rewiring approaches (ICML 2025) and topology-agnostic methods like effective resistance sparsification. The paper only compares against DIGL and +FA, which are not the most recent or comprehensive baselines. This limits the ability to assess whether SDRF's improvements are truly significant relative to the state-of-the-art. The revision should include comparisons with at least 2-3 recent methods from 2022-2025 that specifically target over-squashing mitigation.

- Missing analysis of rewiring impact on graph properties: While the paper claims SDRF preserves graph structure better than DIGL (Table 3, Figure 4), it lacks analysis of how rewiring affects other crucial graph properties beyond degree distribution. Specifically, there's no examination of: (1) how community structure is preserved/altered, (2) changes in shortest path distributions which directly affect message passing depth requirements, (3) impact on graph spectral properties beyond the Cheeger constant, or (4) whether rewiring introduces artifacts that could affect downstream tasks. This analysis is important because rewiring might solve over-squashing but introduce other problems, and the paper's claims about structural preservation need more comprehensive validation.

- Unclear connection between theoretical bounds and practical performance: The theoretical results (Lemma 1, Theorem 4) provide upper bounds on information propagation, but the paper doesn't establish how tight these bounds are in practice or how they correlate with actual task performance. There's no analysis showing that graphs with more negative curvature (according to Balanced Forman) actually exhibit worse over-squashing in the experiments, nor that SDRF's improvements correlate with reductions in negative curvature. This disconnect between the theoretical framework and empirical validation weakens the paper's claim that the curvature-based approach is the right solution. The revision should include analysis showing the relationship between curvature metrics, the Jacobian-based sensitivity measure, and task performance.

- Missing worked example of curvature computation: The paper introduces the Balanced Forman curvature (Definition 1) but never computes it for a concrete, fully-specified graph beyond the brief table entries. This makes it difficult for readers to develop intuition about how the curvature behaves in practice. The paper should include a detailed computation for a small but non-trivial graph, such as a 6-node cycle with one chord, showing step-by-step how each term in equation (3) is calculated. This would help readers understand the interplay between triangles, 4-cycles, and the degeneracy factor γ_max, and verify that the curvature behaves as expected for mixed positive/negative curvature regions.

- No demonstration of over-squashing quantification: While Lemma 1 provides a theoretical bound on the Jacobian, the paper never actually computes or visualizes the Jacobian to demonstrate over-squashing in practice. The paper should include a concrete example showing how the Jacobian entries decay with distance on a graph with known bottlenecks (e.g., two cliques connected by a single edge). This would directly validate the theoretical connection between graph topology and information distortion. Computing the Jacobian for a simple 2-layer GCN on such a graph would make the over-squashing phenomenon tangible and show how the bound in Lemma 2 relates to actual sensitivity.

- Missing synthetic graph validation of curvature effects: The experiments only use real-world datasets where the relationship between curvature and performance is confounded by many factors. The paper needs controlled experiments on synthetic graphs where the ground truth about bottlenecks and long-range dependencies is known. For instance, the paper should test SDRF on synthetic graphs with varying numbers of negatively curved bridge edges and measure both the Jacobian-based sensitivity and task performance. This would directly validate Theorem 4's claim that negatively curved edges cause over-squashing, by showing that removing or rewiring these specific edges improves information flow more than rewiring random edges.

- No guidance on practical implementation choices: Algorithm 1 (SDRF) contains several hyperparameters (τ, C+, max iterations) but the paper provides no guidance on how to choose them in practice. The experimental section mentions optimizing hyperparameters by random search but doesn't report the chosen values or their sensitivity. The paper should include an ablation study showing how performance varies with these choices and provide practical recommendations. For example, how does the temperature τ affect the trade-off between exploration and exploitation in edge addition? What range of C+ values works well across different graph types? This implementation guidance is essential for practitioners to apply the method effectively.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Reframe the central claims about negative curvature to clarify it's sufficient but not necessary for over-squashing, acknowledging limitations

- Expand experimental validation to include diverse tasks requiring long-range dependencies, multiple GNN architectures, and recent baseline methods

- Add analysis measuring actual information distortion (using Jacobian metrics) to empirically validate theoretical claims

- Include comprehensive analysis of how SDRF affects various graph properties beyond degree distribution

- Establish clear connections between theoretical bounds, curvature measurements, and practical task performance

comments.title: Notation conflict in normalized adjacency matrix
comments.quote: We denote the adjacency matrix by $A$ and let $\hat{A}=A+I$ be the adjacency matrix augmented with self-loops. Similarly we let $\hat{D}=D+I$, with $D$ the diagonal degree matrix, and let $\hat{A}=\hat{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}}\hat{A}\hat{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ be the normalized augmented adjacency matrix
comments.explanation: The symbol $\hat{A}$ is defined twice with conflicting meanings: first as $\hat{A}=A+I$, then immediately as $\hat{A}=\hat{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}}\hat{A}\hat{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}}$. This creates a circular definition where $\hat{A}$ appears on both sides of its own definition. Rewrite as: "let $\hat{A}=A+I$ be the adjacency matrix augmented with self-loops... and let $\tilde{A}=\hat{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}}\hat{A}\hat{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ be the normalized augmented adjacency matrix" because the current notation is mathematically inconsistent.

comments.title: Duplicate minimization in Cheeger constant definition
comments.quote: $h_{G}:=\min_{S\subset V}h_{S},\quad h_{S}:=\min_{S\subset V}\frac{|\partial S|}{\min\{\textup{vol}(S),\textup{vol}(V\setminus S)\}}$ (5)
comments.explanation: The definition contains a duplicate minimization over $S\subset V$. The first line defines $h_G$ as the minimum of $h_S$ over all subsets $S$, but then $h_S$ is also defined with a minimization over the same domain. This is redundant and mathematically incorrect. Rewrite equation (5) as "$h_{G}:=\min_{S\subset V}\frac{|\partial S|}{\min\{\textup{vol}(S),\textup{vol}(V\setminus S)\}}$ (5)" because the current formulation is mathematically inconsistent.

comments.title: Missing degree condition in Theorem 4
comments.quote:
Consider a MPNN as in equation 1. Let $i\sim j$ with $d_{i}\leq d_{j}$ and assume that:

- $|\nabla\phi_{\ell}|\leq\alpha$ and $|\nabla\psi_{\ell}|\leq\beta$ for each $0\leq\ell\leq L-1$, with $L\geq 2$ the depth of the MPNN.
- There exists $\delta$ s.t. $0<\delta<(\max\{d_{i},d_{j}\})^{-\frac{1}{2}}$, $\delta<\gamma_{\text{max}}^{-1}$, and $\textup{Ric}(i,j)\leq-2+\delta$.
comments.explanation: The theorem statement is missing an explicit degree condition. Definition 1 sets Ric(i,j) = 0 if min{d_i, d_j} = 1, and the formula requires min{d_i, d_j} > 1 to be well-defined. The proof of Theorem 4 uses properties of 4-cycles and triangles that require both nodes to have degree at least 2, and the bound on |Q_j| > δ^(-1) relies on having sufficient neighbors to form the required structures. Add "with d_i ≥ 2 and d_j ≥ 2" after "Let i∼j with d_i ≤ d_j" because the curvature definition and proof require both nodes to have degree at least 2.

comments.title: Incorrect implication about degree bounds
comments.quote: Secondly, we also point out that $\text{Ric}(i,j)<-2+\delta$ implies that $\min\{d_{i},d_{j}\}>2/\delta$.
comments.explanation: The claimed implication from curvature to degree bound is incorrect. From the Balanced Forman curvature definition, the curvature depends on local topology including triangles and 4-cycles through the $\gamma_{\max}$ term. Consider a counterexample: two nodes with degrees $d_i=d_j=3$ connected by an edge, where each node participates in many 4-cycles. The negative term can make $\text{Ric}(i,j)$ arbitrarily negative while $\min\{d_i,d_j\}=3$ remains fixed, contradicting the claimed bound. Remove the sentence because the implication does not follow from the curvature definition.

comments.title: Contradiction in bottleneck characterization
comments.quote: This phenomenon, referred to as 'over-squashing', has been heuristically attributed to graph bottlenecks where the number of  $k$ -hop neighbors grows rapidly with  $k$ .
comments.explanation: The abstract characterizes bottlenecks as occurring "where the number of k-hop neighbors grows rapidly with k," but this describes expansion rather than bottlenecking. A bottleneck should be characterized by a constriction where information flow is limited, typically where the number of k-hop neighbors grows slowly or becomes constrained. The paper's own theoretical framework (Theorem 4) shows that bottlenecks occur at negatively curved edges, which geometrically represent constrictions in the graph structure. Rewrite this sentence to correctly characterize bottlenecks as regions where information flow is constrained, such as "where the number of k-hop neighbors grows slowly or becomes constrained with k."

comments.title: Incorrect curvature bound claim
comments.quote: To this aim, we introduce a new combinatorial edge-based curvature called Balanced Forman curvature that constitutes a sharp lower bound to the standard Ollivier curvature on graphs
comments.explanation: The claim that Balanced Forman curvature is a sharp lower bound to Ollivier curvature is incorrect. According to Theorem 2 in Section 3, the paper actually proves κ(i,j) ≥ Ric(i,j), meaning Balanced Forman curvature (Ric) is a lower bound to κ(i,j), not to Ollivier curvature. The text in Section 3 states that κ(i,j) is biased towards negative curvature and has richer theory, but never claims it equals Ollivier curvature. This misrepresents the theoretical contribution. Rewrite the sentence as 'To this aim, we introduce a new combinatorial edge-based curvature called Balanced Forman curvature that constitutes a sharp lower bound to the curvature κ(i,j) introduced in Section 3' because the paper only proves this relationship, not one to Ollivier curvature.

comments.title: Proposition 5 lower bound appears too strong
comments.quote: If $\textup{Ric}(i,j)\geq k>0$ for all $i\sim j$, then $\lambda_{1}/2\geq h_{G}\geq\frac{k}{2}$.
comments.explanation: The lower bound $h_G \geq k/2$ appears too strong given the definition of the Cheeger constant. Consider a graph with two dense communities connected by a single edge, where all edges have Ricci curvature $k=1$. The Cheeger constant would be $h_G = \frac{1}{\min\{\text{vol}(S), \text{vol}(\bar{S})\}}$, which can be arbitrarily small if the communities are large. This contradicts the claimed lower bound $h_G \geq 1/2$. The proof needs to clarify what additional assumptions or definitions of Ricci curvature are being used to obtain this bound.

comments.title: Unjustified iteration bound for SDRF
comments.quote: We can do this with SDRF, with the maximum number of iterations set to $\rho/2$.
comments.explanation: The claim that setting iterations to $\rho/2$ ensures graph-edit distance constraint is unjustified. Each SDRF iteration can modify multiple edges through edge additions and removals. The graph-edit distance $\rho$ counts total edge modifications, not iterations. If each iteration modifies $m$ edges on average, the total edit distance would be approximately $m \times (\rho/2)$, potentially exceeding $\rho$. The paper needs to either specify that each iteration modifies exactly one edge or provide a bound accounting for edge modifications per iteration. Add clarification after the quoted sentence: "where each iteration of SDRF modifies at most one edge, ensuring the total edit distance remains within $\rho$".

comments.title: Inconsistent performance ranking in heterophilic datasets
comments.explanation: The text claims that curvature-based approaches like SDRF should perform better on low-homophily datasets, but the table shows that for Cornell (0.11 homophily), DIGL + Undirected achieves 59.54% while SDRF + Undirected achieves only 57.54%. This contradicts the stated expectation that SDRF should outperform diffusion methods on heterophilic datasets. The authors should either revise their claim to acknowledge this exception or provide an explanation for why Cornell behaves differently from the theoretical expectation.

comments.title: Unexplained performance degradation on Squirrel dataset
comments.explanation: The table shows that DIGL + Undirected performs significantly worse (33.36%) than the baseline (39.83%) on the Squirrel dataset, which is unexpected given that Squirrel has moderate homophily (0.22). The paper's explanation about diffusion-based methods performing poorly on low-homophily datasets doesn't fully account for this dramatic drop on a moderately homophilic dataset. The authors should provide additional analysis explaining why Squirrel shows this anomalous behavior or acknowledge this as a limitation of their theoretical explanation.

comments.title: Missing proof of Lemma 1
comments.quote:
###### Lemma 1.

Assume an MPNN as in equation 1. Let $i,s\in V$ with $s\in S_{r+1}(i)$. If $|\nabla\phi_{\ell}|\leq\alpha$ and $|\nabla\psi_{\ell}|\leq\beta$ for $0\leq\ell\leq r$, then

$\left|\frac{\partial h_{i}^{(r+1)}}{\partial x_{s}}\right|\leq(\alpha\beta)^{r+1}(\hat{A}^{r+1})_{is}.$ (2)
comments.explanation: The statement of Lemma 1 is provided but no proof is given. The lemma claims a specific bound on the Jacobian in terms of powers of the augmented normalized adjacency matrix $\hat{A}$. Without a proof, it's unclear how the $(\hat{A}^{r+1})_{is}$ term emerges from the message passing dynamics. Add a proof showing how repeated application of the chain rule through $r+1$ layers yields the $(\alpha\beta)^{r+1}$ factor and how the adjacency matrix power captures the propagation paths.

comments.title: Jacobian bound requires Lipschitz constants
comments.quote:
If $|\nabla\phi_{\ell}|\leq\alpha$ and $|\nabla\psi_{\ell}|\leq\beta$ for $0\leq\ell\leq r$, then

$\left|\frac{\partial h_{i}^{(r+1)}}{\partial x_{s}}\right|\leq(\alpha\beta)^{r+1}(\hat{A}^{r+1})_{is}.$ (2)
comments.explanation:
The notation $|
abla\phi_{\ell}|\leq\alpha$ is ambiguous. For the Jacobian bound in equation (2) to hold through the chain rule across $r+1$ layers, $|
abla\phi_{\ell}|$ and $|
abla\psi_{\ell}|$ must be interpreted as operator norms (spectral norms) that bound the Lipschitz constants of the functions. Pointwise gradient bounds are insufficient to guarantee the multiplicative factor $(\alpha eta)^{r+1}$ in the operator norm of the composed Jacobian. Rewrite the condition as "$\|
abla\phi_{\ell}\|_2 \leq \alpha$ and $\|
abla\psi_{\ell}\|_2 \leq  eta$ for $0\leq\ell\leq r$" to clarify that these are Lipschitz constants.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in curvature formula
comments.quote: ame {R i c} (i, j) := \frac {2}{d _ {i}} + \frac {2}{d _ {j}} - 2 + 2 \frac {\left| \sharp_ {\Delta} (i , j) \right|}{\max  \left\{d _ {i} , d _ {j} \right\}} + \frac {\left| \sharp_ {\Delta} (i , j) \right|}{\min  \left\{d _ {i} , d _ {j} \right\}} + \frac {\left(\gamma_ {\max }\right) ^ {- 1}}{\max  \left\{d _ {i} , d _ {j} \right\}} \left(\left| \sharp_ {\square} ^ {i} \right| + \left| \sharp_ {\square} ^ {j} \right|\right), \tag {3}
comments.explanation: The notation in the formula is inconsistent with the definitions. The term γ_max appears without arguments (i,j), while it was defined as γ_max(i,j). Similarly, ♯_□^i and ♯_□^j appear without the edge argument (i,j), contradicting definition (ii) where these sets explicitly depend on the edge. This creates ambiguity in the formula. Rewrite the last term as "\frac{\left(\gamma_{\max}(i,j)\right)^{-1}}{\max \left\{d _ {i} , d _ {j} \right\}} \left(\left| \sharp_ {\square} ^ {i}(i,j) \right| + \left| \sharp_ {\square} ^ {j}(i,j) \right|\right)" to maintain consistent notation.

comments.title: PPR matrix definition needs clarification
comments.quote: $R_{\alpha}:=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\theta_{k}^{PPR}(D^{-1}A)^{k}=\alpha\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\left((1-\alpha)(D^{-1}A)\right)^{k}.$
comments.explanation: The PPR matrix definition uses undefined coefficients $\theta_{k}^{PPR}$ in the first expression. The second expression correctly shows $\theta_{k}^{PPR} = \alpha(1-\alpha)^k$, which matches the standard PPR definition $R_{\alpha} = \alpha\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}((1-\alpha)D^{-1}A)^k$. The inconsistency is that the first expression introduces undefined notation. Rewrite as: '$R_{\alpha} := \alpha\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}((1-\alpha)D^{-1}A)^{k}$' and remove the undefined $\theta_{k}^{PPR}$ notation.

comments.title: Inconsistent edge removal reporting in Table 3
comments.explanation: Table 3 shows inconsistent reporting of edge removals for DIGL. The table header indicates '% edges added / removed by method', but DIGL reports 0.0% removals for Cornell, Texas, Wisconsin, and Citeseer, while reporting non-zero removals for other datasets (11.8% for Chameleon, 1.9% for Squirrel, etc.). This inconsistency needs clarification. If DIGL is a purely edge-addition method as suggested by the 0.0% entries for several datasets, then the non-zero removal percentages for other datasets should be explained, or the table should be corrected to reflect DIGL's actual behavior across all datasets.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper attempts to provide a rigorous geometric explanation for over-squashing in GNNs by introducing Balanced Forman curvature and a rewiring method. While the theoretical framework is ambitious, it suffers from a critical contradiction in the curvature definition regarding bridges and lacks direct experimental validation of the proposed theoretical mechanism. The experimental results focus on downstream task performance rather than demonstrating the reduction of over-squashing as defined in the analysis.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by attempting to formalize the relationship between graph topology and information flow limitations. The introduction of Balanced Forman curvature offers a new combinatorial tool, though it currently fails to identify the most obvious bottleneck structures (bridges). The theoretical analysis provides a local condition for over-squashing, but the experimental section focuses solely on node classification accuracy rather than validating the theoretical mechanism.

Overview issues:

- Curvature Definition Contradicts Theoretical Claims Regarding Bridges: The paper claims that 'the curvature is negative when i~j behaves as a bridge' and that negatively curved edges are responsible for over-squashing (Section 3). However, the definition of Balanced Forman curvature explicitly sets Ric(i,j) to zero if min{d_i, d_j} = 1 (Equation 3). Since a bridge connects nodes of degree 1, the curvature is defined as zero, not negative. This directly contradicts the text's interpretation of the metric. Bridges are the textbook definition of graph bottlenecks, so excluding them from the curvature calculation undermines the paper's core theoretical claim that curvature identifies the edges causing over-squashing. The authors must either adjust the definition to capture bridges or provide a rigorous justification for why bridges do not contribute to over-squashing despite being structural bottlenecks.

comments.title: Incorrect characterization of small-world graphs
comments.quote: In many cases however (e.g. in ‘small-world’ graphs such as social networks), the size of the receptive field $B_{r}(i)$ grows exponentially with $r$.
comments.explanation: Small-world graphs are defined by shortcuts that lead to a small diameter and polynomial neighborhood growth. In standard models like Watts-Strogatz, the size of the receptive field $B_r(i)$ grows polynomially, not exponentially. The exponential growth you describe is typical of 'large-world' graphs, such as trees or grids. Using social networks as an example of exponential neighborhood growth is factually incorrect and misrepresents the definition of small-world graphs.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a new edge-based combinatorial curvature, 'Balanced Forman curvature,' to characterize and mitigate the over-squashing phenomenon in GNNs via a rewiring technique called Stochastic Discrete Ricci Flow (SDRF).

The paper provides a novel geometric interpretation of GNN bottlenecks, effectively linking negative curvature to information decay. The theoretical framework is well-motivated, and the empirical results demonstrate that curvature-based rewiring can improve performance, particularly on low-homophily datasets.

Overview issues:

- Unclear Link Between Curvature and Task-Relevant Information: The paper assumes that curvature-based bottlenecks are the primary cause of poor performance in long-range dependency tasks. However, it does not explicitly demonstrate that the edges identified as 'negatively curved' are the same ones where task-relevant information is lost. Theorem 4 provides a bound on the Jacobian of node representations, but this is a general property of the graph structure rather than a task-specific metric. To strengthen the paper, the authors should include an analysis or visualization showing that the edges modified by SDRF actually correspond to the paths carrying the most critical information for the downstream classification task.

- Sensitivity of Theoretical Bounds to Graph Density: Corollary 3 and Theorem 4 rely on the assumption that curvature is the primary driver of the receptive field growth. In real-world graphs with heterogeneous degree distributions, the degree of nodes often plays a more significant role in message-passing volume than local curvature. The bounds provided in Section 3 do not account for how degree variance might dominate the over-squashing effect independently of the curvature metric. The authors should clarify the conditions under which their curvature-based bound is tighter than a simple degree-based bound, perhaps by evaluating the performance on synthetic graphs with controlled degree distributions.

- Potential for Structural Artifacts in Rewiring: The SDRF rewiring method (Algorithm 1) adds and removes edges based on local curvature, which risks disrupting the global structural properties of the graph. While the authors argue that the modifications are 'surgical,' they do not quantify the impact on spectral properties beyond the Cheeger constant. Given the importance of spectral alignment in GNNs, it is important to show that the rewiring does not introduce artifacts that lead to over-smoothing or other unintended effects. Expanding the analysis in Section 4 to include a study of the spectral gap before and after SDRF would provide a more complete picture of the structural impact.

- Lack of Comparison with State-of-the-Art Structural Baselines: The experimental section compares SDRF against DIGL and a simple +FA baseline. However, the field has seen several recent developments in GNN rewiring, such as DropEdge or spectral-preserving methods, which are not included in the evaluation. The current comparison is limited to methods that may not be the most effective at preserving global graph topology. Including a broader set of baselines, particularly those that specifically target long-range dependencies without aggressive graph densification, would better establish the competitiveness of the proposed approach.

- Need for a controlled synthetic experiment on over-squashing: The paper relies on a theoretical link between curvature and the Jacobian of node representations but lacks a controlled synthetic experiment to verify that the over-squashing effect is directly mitigated by the proposed curvature-based rewiring. A standard approach would be to construct a 'bottlenecked' graph (e.g., two large clusters connected by a single path or a tree-like structure) and demonstrate that SDRF successfully increases the sensitivity of node representations across the bottleneck compared to the original graph. Without such a demonstration, it remains unclear whether the observed performance gains on real-world datasets are due to the mitigation of over-squashing or other factors like improved local connectivity.

- Lack of empirical analysis on the trade-off between rewiring and over-smoothing: The paper addresses over-squashing but provides no evidence that the proposed rewiring does not exacerbate the related problem of over-smoothing. Since SDRF adds edges to the graph, it effectively increases the connectivity, which is a known driver of over-smoothing in GNNs. The paper should include an analysis of the spectral properties or the feature alignment (e.g., Dirichlet energy) of the rewired graphs to demonstrate that the benefit of reduced over-squashing is not negated by increased over-smoothing, particularly as the number of GNN layers increases.

Recommendation: minor revision. The paper is conceptually strong and well-written, but it requires a more rigorous justification of the link between local curvature and global task performance. Addressing the impact of rewiring on spectral properties and including more robust baselines would significantly increase the impact of the work.

Revision targets:

- Include a sensitivity analysis or simulation to demonstrate that the curvature-based bottlenecks identified by the model are indeed the primary source of information loss for specific downstream tasks.

- Perform a spectral analysis of the rewired graphs to ensure that the structural modifications do not negatively impact the graph's fundamental spectral properties.

- Expand the experimental evaluation to include at least one additional state-of-the-art graph rewiring baseline that is not purely diffusion-based.

comments.title: Inconsistency in the definition of bottleneck growth
comments.quote: This phenomenon, referred to as 'over-squashing', has been heuristically attributed to graph bottlenecks where the number of  $k$ -hop neighbors grows rapidly with  $k$
comments.explanation: The description of bottlenecks as regions where the number of k-hop neighbors grows rapidly is counter-intuitive. In graph theory, bottlenecks typically correspond to regions where the number of neighbors grows slowly, or where the boundary of a set of nodes is small relative to the set itself. If the neighborhood grew rapidly, the graph would be highly expansive, which is the opposite of a bottleneck.
